title: JVM & Performance
description: Class loading, JIT compilation, GC selection, memory areas, and diagnosing performance problems in a running JVM.

questions.question: Describe the JVM class loading process: loading, linking (verification, preparation, resolution) and initialization. What is the class loader delegation model and what problem does it prevent?
questions.explanation:
Class loading has three phases. Loading: a class loader finds the .class bytes and creates the Class object. Linking: verification (bytecode is structurally valid and type-safe), preparation (static fields allocated, set to defaults — 0/null, not your values), resolution (symbolic references to other classes/methods replaced with direct ones, often lazily). Initialization: static initializers and static field assignments run — only on first active use (new, static method call, static field access).

class A {
  static int x = 42;   // preparation: x = 0;  initialization: x = 42
  static { System.out.println("A init"); }  // first use, not JVM startup
}

Delegation model: each loader asks its parent first (application → platform → bootstrap); only if the parent can't find the class does the child load it itself. This prevents spoofing core classes — a java.lang.String on your classpath can never replace the real one, because the bootstrap loader finds it first. Bonus: the same class name loaded by two different loaders yields two distinct classes — the basis of application-server and plugin isolation.

questions.question: Java is 'compiled to bytecode, then JIT-compiled'. Explain tiered compilation: what do the interpreter, C1 and C2 each contribute, and what is deoptimization — when does the JVM throw compiled code away?
questions.explanation:
Tiered compilation: code starts slow and gets faster as the JVM learns about it.

  • Interpreter: executes bytecode immediately, zero compile cost; counts invocations and loop iterations, gathers first profile data.
  • C1 (client compiler): once a method warms up, produces quick, lightly-optimized machine code with profiling counters baked in (branch frequencies, observed receiver types).
  • C2 (server compiler): for genuinely hot methods, compiles aggressively using C1's profile — deep inlining, escape analysis, speculative devirtualization.

Deoptimization: C2 code relies on assumptions ('only one implementation of this interface is loaded', 'this branch is never taken'). When one breaks, the JVM hits an uncommon trap: it throws the compiled code away, falls back to the interpreter, reprofiles and recompiles.

Shape s = next();
s.area();   // only Circle loaded for months → C2 inlines Circle.area()
// a plugin loads Square → assumption invalid → deopt, recompile with real dispatch

questions.question: Map out the JVM's memory areas: heap, thread stacks, metaspace, code cache, and direct (off-heap) memory. For each, name one error or symptom you see when it is exhausted.
questions.explanation:
Five memory areas, each with its own failure signature:

  • Heap — objects. Exhausted: OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space (often preceded by rising GC time / "GC overhead limit exceeded"). Tune: -Xmx.
  • Thread stacks — one per thread, holds frames and locals. Deep recursion: StackOverflowError; too many threads: OutOfMemoryError: unable to create native thread. Tune: -Xss.
  • Metaspace — class metadata, native memory. Classloader leaks (hot redeploys): OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace. Tune: -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize.
  • Code cache — JIT-compiled machine code. Full: log warning CodeCache is full. Compiler has been disabled — no crash, the app silently degrades to interpreter speed.
  • Direct (off-heap) — ByteBuffer.allocateDirect, NIO, Netty. Exhausted: OutOfMemoryError: Direct buffer memory; invisible in heap dumps. Tune: -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize.

Takeaway: "OutOfMemoryError" alone says little — the message names the area, and each area has a completely different fix.

questions.question: What is escape analysis and what optimizations does it enable (scalar replacement, lock elision)? Why can allocating short-lived objects in a hot loop still be effectively free?
questions.explanation:
Escape analysis: the JIT (C2) proves an object never leaves the scope that created it — not stored to a field, not returned, not passed anywhere that could keep it. A non-escaping object doesn't need to be a real heap object.

  • Scalar replacement: the object is dissolved into its fields, which live in registers/on the stack — no allocation, nothing for the GC.
  • Lock elision: synchronizing on a non-escaping object can't be observed by any other thread, so the lock is removed entirely.
for (int i = 0; i < 1_000_000; i++) {
  Point p = new Point(i, i);  // never escapes the loop body
  sum += p.x + p.y;           // after EA: sum += i + i — no Point ever allocated
}

Why hot-loop short-lived objects are effectively free: either they're scalar-replaced (never allocated at all), or they die young — allocation in the TLAB is a pointer bump, and a young GC only pays for surviving objects, so dead ones cost ~nothing. Caveat: EA works only after inlining succeeds; passing the object to a big non-inlined method defeats it.

questions.question: Compare the modern garbage collectors: G1, ZGC and Shenandoah. What does each optimize for, and how would you choose one for (a) a latency-sensitive API with a 100 GB heap and (b) a small batch job?
questions.explanation:
All three are region-based; they optimize different corners of the throughput / latency / footprint triangle.

  • G1 (default): balances throughput and pause time; young + mixed collections chase a pause target (-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis, default 200 ms). Pauses grow with heap and live set — fine up to tens of GB.
  • ZGC: almost fully concurrent (colored pointers + load barriers); pauses sub-millisecond and independent of heap size — built for 100 GB+ heaps. Costs some throughput and extra CPU/memory. Generational since JDK 21.
  • Shenandoah: same goal as ZGC — concurrent compaction, low pause — different lineage (Red Hat) and mechanics; also strong on modest heaps.

(a) Latency-sensitive API with 100 GB heap → ZGC: G1 pauses would stretch with that live set; ZGC stays sub-ms. (b) Small batch job → pauses are irrelevant, total runtime matters → Parallel GC (max throughput per CPU) or just default G1. Takeaway: choose by SLO — pause-sensitive → ZGC/Shenandoah, throughput → Parallel, balanced default → G1.

questions.question: A service's p99 latency spikes every few minutes while p50 stays flat. Walk through how you would confirm or rule out GC as the cause — which logs/flags and which metrics (pause time vs allocation rate) would you look at?
questions.explanation:
p99 spikes every few minutes with flat p50 smells like stop-the-world pauses — but confirm, don't assume.

  1. Enable GC + safepoint logging: -Xlog:gc*,safepoint:file=gc.log:time,uptime — cheap enough for production.
  2. Correlate timestamps: do GC pauses line up with the latency spikes? Compare pause duration and frequency against the spike pattern — e.g. a 250 ms young GC every ~2 minutes matching each spike = confirmed.
  3. Check the allocation rate (log deltas, jstat -gc 1s, or JFR): high allocation → frequent young GCs; steadily rising old-gen occupancy after GC → promotion pressure, mixed/full GCs, or a leak.
  4. If pauses don't align: read the safepoint log anyway — long time-to-safepoint stalls look exactly like GC but aren't — then look outside the JVM (CPU throttling, noisy neighbor, downstream dependency).

Continuous metrics worth graphing: GC pause percentiles, GC frequency, allocation rate (MB/s), heap occupancy after collection. jcmd PID JFR.start captures all of them with ~1% overhead.

questions.question: Which JDK tools would you use on a live, misbehaving JVM and for what: `jcmd`, `jstack`, `jmap`, `jstat`? Describe how you would diagnose a suspected deadlock and a suspected memory leak with them.

What is Java Flight Recorder (JFR), and why is it safe to run in production? What kinds of events does it capture, and how does it compare to a sampling profiler like async-profiler with flame graphs?
Why do JVM applications start slow, and what do CDS/AppCDS, AOT caching (Project Leyden) and GraalVM native image each do about it? What do you trade away with a native image?

Why are naive Java microbenchmarks (a loop around `System.nanoTime()`) almost always wrong? Explain the pitfalls — warmup, dead-code elimination, on-stack replacement — and how JMH is designed to avoid them.
